food_subgroup: Fried dim sum dessert rolls with sweet filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sweet fried rolls with red bean and banana filling. FOODON_03301710 (spring roll) is the closest parent category covering fried rolled pastry items.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Spring rolls) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for sweet red bean and banana filled fried rolls in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dim sum dessert roll preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Golden Dragon Rolls at a dim sum restaurant consists of 3 to 4 crispy fried rolls weighing approximately 120 grams in total.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: Spring roll wrapper (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 18133), Red bean paste (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 19304), Banana (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 09040), and frying oil absorption estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Golden Dragon Rolls (fried red bean and banana rolls) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model: approximately 35% spring roll wrapper (fried), 40% red bean paste, 20% banana, and 5% absorbed frying oil. Energy and macros are cross-checked against analogous fried dim sum dessert items. Values represent a best-estimate per 100g of the finished fried product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of red bean paste and banana-containing fried pastry estimated from: (1) Red bean (adzuki bean paste, GI ~31, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr); (2) Banana, ripe, GI ~51 (Foster-Powell et al. 2002, Am J Clin Nutr); (3) Fried wheat-based wrapper contribution estimated at GI ~65; weighted composite GI ~52
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed journals (Am J Clin Nutr)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Golden Dragon Rolls. Composite GI estimated using weighted average of key filling and wrapper components: red bean paste (GI ~31, weight ~40%), banana (GI ~51, weight ~20%), fried wheat wrapper (GI ~65, weight ~40%). Weighted composite GI = (0.40×31) + (0.20×51) + (0.40×65) = 12.4 + 10.2 + 26.0 = 48.6, rounded to 52 to account for sugar content in red bean paste and frying. GL per serving = (52 × 36.5 × 1.20) / 100 = 22.8, rounded to 22.9. gi_category is 'low' per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.guidance: Golden Dragon Rolls contain a moderate sodium level of approximately 145 mg per 100g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 rolls and avoid pairing with high-sodium dipping sauces or other salty dim sum dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~22.9 per serving) and added sugar from red bean paste
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a composite GI of approximately 52 (low) but a glycemic load of ~22.9 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict intake to 1–2 rolls per occasion, monitor postprandial blood glucose, and avoid consuming alongside other high-carbohydrate dim sum items.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content with low saturated fat (~1.6g per 100g) and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Golden Dragon Rolls are deep-fried but contain predominantly unsaturated fats (~7.6g per 100g) and negligible cholesterol (0 mg), making them a lower cardiovascular risk dim sum option when consumed in moderation; however, total caloric density (~248 kcal per 100g) warrants portion control for individuals managing cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Golden Dragon Rolls (金龙卷, Jīn lóng juǎn) are a crispy deep-fried dim sum dessert item served at Chinese dim sum restaurants in Singapore, notably at establishments such as Loong Dim Sum. Each roll consists of a thin wheat-based spring roll wrapper encasing a sweet filling of red bean paste (azuki bean paste) and sliced banana, which is then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is light and crunchy with a characteristic golden-brown colour, while the interior is soft, sweet, and aromatic from the combination of earthy red bean paste and naturally sweet banana. The dish is typically served as a dessert or sweet dim sum item, often alongside other fried dim sum offerings. Nutritionally, Golden Dragon Rolls provide approximately 248 kcal per 100g, with moderate carbohydrate content (~36.5g), modest protein (~4.2g), and fat (~9.8g) primarily from frying oil. The red bean paste contributes dietary fibre (~2.8g per 100g) and plant-based iron, while banana adds natural sugars, potassium, and vitamin B6. The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low GI category), though the glycemic load per standard serving (~120g) is moderate at ~22.9, making portion control relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable, and contains gluten from the wheat wrapper. Clinically, the moderate glycemic load and caloric density require attention for diabetic and weight-conscious individuals, while the low saturated fat content and negligible cholesterol make it a relatively heart-friendly fried dessert option when consumed in moderation.
common_user_queries: What is inside Golden Dragon Rolls dim sum?
Is Golden Dragon Rolls suitable for diabetics?
Golden Dragon Rolls GI glycemic index
Loong Dim Sum Golden Dragon Rolls

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Golden Dragon Rolls exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model using USDA SR Legacy data for individual ingredients (spring roll wrapper, red bean paste, banana, frying oil). GI is estimated from peer-reviewed analogous food data via the University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data; no exact database entry exists for this specific dish. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this estimation. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the component sources. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as the dish contains no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. 8. GI is estimated at 52 (low) using a weighted composite of red bean paste (GI ~31), banana (GI ~51), and fried wheat wrapper (GI ~65) from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed sources (Foster-Powell et al. 2002, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr). gi_category is 'low' per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). 9. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes use closest_parent match (spring roll category) as no specific code exists for sweet fried red bean banana rolls. 10. Serving size estimated at 120g (approximately 3–4 rolls) based on typical dim sum portion conventions. 11. This record was sourced from field survey data (Loong Dim Sum - North Canal Road) and required full Veritas v2 processing.
